macro_segments.title: Three Explanations for Liability in Stolen Sanctified Animals: Introduction and Framing

micro_segments.title: The core problem: How can one be liable for four and five if the animal cannot be eaten after slaughter?
Rabbi Yochanan's first answer: Slaughtered in the Temple for the owner's sake, with blood subsequently spilled
Rabbi Yochanan's second answer: Slaughtered in the Temple not for the owner's sake (shelo lishmo)
Reish Lakish's answer: Slaughtered outside the Temple with a blemish, allowing redemption

macro_segments.title: Investigating the Validity of Slaughter When Secondary Acts Are Incomplete: The Doctrine of Kol Ha'omed

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar's challenge: How can slaughter be valid if it requires additional acts (blood sprinkling or redemption) to make meat edible?
Introduction to Rabbi Shimon's principle: 'Kol ha'omed lizrok kezrok dami' – fitness for blood-sprinkling is as if it were done
Illustrative case from Baraita: Leftover meat of a sacrifice and when it achieves the status of food
Analysis of notar that slept over before blood-sprinkling versus after: application of the potential-act rule
Eliciting the principle: 'Kol ha'omed lizrok kezrok dami' – meat is deemed food if it had an opportunity for the blood to be thrown

macro_segments.title: Extension of Kol Ha'omed to Redemption: The Case of a Para Aduma with a Blemish

micro_segments.title: Baraita regarding para aduma: Can it contract food-related impurity if it had an opportunity to be redeemed?
Reish Lakish's teaching: A para aduma can be redeemed even while sitting on the pyre, up until the blood is sprinkled
Principle extended: 'Kol ha'omed lifdos k'padui dami' – fitness for redemption is as if it were redeemed
Application to Reish Lakish's answer: A blemished animal slaughtered outside the Temple qualifies as valid slaughter because it could theoretically be redeemed

macro_segments.title: Debate Between Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish: Why Each Rejected the Other's Scenario

micro_segments.title: Why Rabbi Yochanan did not invoke Reish Lakish's blemished-animal scenario: desire to apply the rule even to unblemished animals
Introduction of the principle 'Tvicha u-mechira': slaughter and sale must be parallel – one liable implies the other
Application to unblemished sanctified animals: Cannot be sold, therefore cannot incur liability for slaughter
Summary: Both sages follow their respective principles regarding the relationship between slaughter and sale
Reish Lakish's position: Tvicha and mechira are interdependent – liability for one requires liability for the other

macro_segments.title: Testing the Tvicha u-Mechira Principle: The Case of a Treifa Animal

micro_segments.title: Baraita: One who steals and sells a treifa animal pays four and five – surprising if slaughter of treifa is exempt (Rabbi Shimon's view)
Rabbi Yochanan's challenge: This baraita suggests Rabbi Shimon's position – if so, why does it discuss only selling, not slaughtering a treifa?
Reish Lakish's weak response: The baraita mentions only one scenario per case, but both apply
Gemara's counterargument: If the baraita follows the Rabbanan, why separate the cases and mention different scenarios for each?
Impasse: The baraita's structure remains unexplained, leaving the debate unresolved

macro_segments.title: Kilayim (Crossbred Animals) in the Law of Theft: Interpreting the Word 'She' (Sheep) and Multiple Or's

micro_segments.title: Establishing kilayim as subject to the four-and-five penalty: Torah says 'ox or sheep' but includes crossbreeds
Rava's principle: The word 'she' by itself excludes crossbreeds, but context determines whether an 'or' includes or excludes them
In theft law: 'Ox or she' implies no crossbreeds (ox and sheep cannot interbreed), so the 'or' comes to include kilayim
In kodshim law: 'Ox or lamb or goat' suggests crossbreeds possible (sheep and goat can interbreed), so the 'or's exclude both kilayim and nidmeh (different-looking animals)
Why two 'or's in kodshim: If 'or' came to include, only one would suffice; but if it excludes, the first excludes kilayim and the second excludes nidmeh
Gemara's unresolved question: Does Rava's principle of default meaning ever actually apply, given the context-dependent analysis?

topical_tags.term: Kol ha'omed lizrok kezrok dami (potential for blood-sprinkling counts as if done)
Kol ha'omed lifdos k'padui dami (potential for redemption counts as if done)
Tvicha u-mechira (parallel liability for slaughter and sale)
Nidmeh (animal that looks different from parents)
